text: The two angels arrived at Sodom in the evening; Lot was sitting at the city gate and rose to meet them, bowing down.
He urged them to stay at his house for the night and wash their feet; they said they would rather spend the night in the square, but he insisted.
Lot pressed them strongly, so they went with him; he prepared a feast with unleavened bread and they ate.
Before they lay down, the men of the city, young and old, surrounded the house.
They called to Lot, demanding he bring out the men so they could abuse them.
Lot went out to them, shutting the door behind him.
He begged them not to act so wickedly.
He offered his two virgin daughters instead, asking them to leave the visitors alone, since they had come under his roof's protection.
The mob told him to get out of the way, and pressed hard against Lot, moving to break down the door.
The two visitors reached out, pulled Lot back inside, and shut the door.
They struck the men at the door, young and old, with blindness, so they wearied themselves trying to find the door.
The men asked Lot if he had anyone else in the city — sons, daughters, or sons-in-law — to bring them out.
They were about to destroy the place, because the outcry against it had grown great before the LORD.
Lot warned his daughters' intended husbands to flee, but they thought he was joking.
At dawn the angels urged Lot to hurry, take his wife and daughters, or be swept away in the city's punishment.
As he hesitated, the men seized his hand, his wife's, and his daughters' hands and led them out of the city, the LORD being merciful to him.
Once outside, one said, escape for your life; do not look back or stop anywhere on the plain; flee to the hills.
Lot said, oh no, my lords.
He said he had found favor, but feared he could not escape to the hills before disaster overtook him.
He asked to flee instead to a small nearby town, so his life might be spared.
The angel granted this request too, and would not overthrow that town.
He told Lot to hurry there, since nothing could be done until he arrived; so the town was named Zoar.
The sun had risen over the land when Lot reached Zoar.
Then the LORD rained down fire and sulfur on Sodom and Gomorrah out of the heavens.
He overthrew those cities and the whole plain, along with everyone living there and everything growing on the ground.
But Lot's wife looked back from behind him, and she became a pillar of salt.
Early the next morning Abraham went to the place where he had stood before the LORD.
He looked toward Sodom and Gomorrah and the whole plain, and saw smoke rising like from a furnace.
When God destroyed the cities of the plain, he remembered Abraham and sent Lot out of the destruction.
Lot left Zoar and lived in the hills with his two daughters, afraid to stay in Zoar, settling in a cave.
The older daughter told the younger there was no man left to give them children as was the custom everywhere.
She proposed they get their father drunk with wine and sleep with him to preserve his line.
That night they made him drink wine, and the older daughter slept with him without his knowledge.
The next day the older told the younger they should do the same again that night, so both could preserve their father's line.
So they made him drink wine again that night, and the younger slept with him without his knowledge.
Both of Lot's daughters became pregnant by their father.
The older bore a son named Moab, ancestor of the Moabites to this day.
The younger also bore a son, named Ben-ammi, ancestor of the Ammonites to this day.
